summary: Validate all miniapp functionality + oracle lanes (VRF, confidential, data) for correctness/robustness/fault-recovery/efficiency

apps.how: encrypt to oracle pubkey, store on MiniAppMessageEVM, reveal in TEE
encrypt payload to oracle pubkey, confidential store, TEE release
reads price plus dispatches automation.upkeep via edge
NeoX requests Morpheus VRF; N3 path GetRandom not oracle
USD via shared price over MorpheusDataFeed
builder only, fetch on lane later

lanes.evidence:
TRACE (both chains): app calls kernel requestFromCallback(requester,"random.generate",payload) [MiniAppDiceGameEVM.sol:108] -> RequestQueued -> relayer discovers via block-scan+request-cursor reconcile (request-processor.js) -> resolveKernelIntent maps to moduleId random.generate / workerRoute /vrf/random [router.js:181-188] -> COMPUTE: in-TEE handleVrf uses crypto.getRandomValues(32) [vrf.js:6] (flag MORPHEUS_RELAYER_ENCLAVE_FULFILL ON), or relayer-local crypto.randomBytes(32) [fulfillment.js:918] (flag OFF, the DEFAULT per config.js:438) -> SIGN by oracle_verifier over a digest that BINDS the randomness (N3 sha256(resultBytes) [router.js:319-321,360-374]; EVM keccak256(resultBytesHex) [neox.js:359, MorpheusOracleEVM.sol:146-150]) -> fulfillRequest; contract re-derives digest + verifies sig against oracleVerifier [MorpheusOracleEVM.sol:153-168].

LIVE ON-CHAIN (queried today via eth_call/invokefunction):
- Neo X mainnet kernel 0xeCFC1C652B5cCdBfe3E9314a83156787D92a3fD2 @block 6816085: totalRequests=30, oracleVerifier==updater==0x622ae03b...; enumerated all 30 requests: 27 random.generate ALL status=Succeeded, each a distinct 32-byte result (apps vrf-e2e #1-7, dice-evm #9-13, dice #14-28). Statistical: 27/27 DISTINCT, bit-balance 50.4% ones over 6912 bits, first-byte spread 24..248 => unbiased CSPRNG, NOT Math.random.
- Neo N3 mainnet kernel 0xf54d8584ef82315c1800373272ab08ae0db2d5ef: getTotalRequests=7, getTotalFulfilled=6; VRF requests #2,#3(miniapp-dice-game/vrf_random),#4,#5 all Succeeded with distinct 32B randomness (e.g. c0d552ee..., aacc53bf...); #1 left Pending (first test req).
- Public verification key endpoint returns verification_public_key 0x03399c42... (matches in-TEE oracle_verifier ground truth).

TESTS: vrf.test.mjs + enclave-server.test.mjs = 31/31 pass; fulfillment.test.mjs = 79/79 pass, incl explicit "flag-ON VRF uses the enclave (no relayer-local randomBytes; raw 32B from enclave)", "flag-OFF keeps two-step host path", and digest cross-check pass for neo_n3/legacy/neox.
apps_using: dice-game (Neo N3 miniapp-dice-game/vrf_random, fulfilled on N3 mainnet #3)
dice-evm / dice (Neo X EVM MiniAppDiceGameEVM, 20 fulfilled VRF requests on Neo X mainnet)
vrf-e2e (oracle self-test app, fulfilled on both N3 and Neo X)

on-chain-tarot / fogplay-coinflip / red-envelope / last-survivor / burn-league / gasbox (per memory these use NeoVM-native Runtime.GetRandom in their own self-contained contracts, NOT the oracle VRF lane — their contracts are in a separate repo and not exercised by this oracle)
lanes.correctness: Randomness generation is correct and the output is consensus-bound. The 32-byte CSPRNG output is committed by the oracle_verifier signature: N3 digest hashes sha256(resultBytes) [router.js:360-374], EVM digest hashes keccak256(resultBytesHex) [neox.js:359] matching the on-chain MorpheusOracleEVM.fulfillmentDigest [.sol:146-150]; the relayer also independently recomputes the digest and REFUSES to submit on mismatch [fulfillment.js:509-520]. The contract re-derives the same digest and recovers/verifies oracleVerifier, so a tampered result fails on-chain. The 32-byte randomness reaches the contract intact via the compact-callback path (resolveCompactCallbackBytes returns the raw bytes for rng/vrf/random [router.js:109-117]; resolveResultBytesHex [neox.js:324-329]). Reference consumer MiniAppDiceGameEVM derives the die as uint8(uint256(keccak256(result))%6)+1 [.sol:155] — re-hashing then mod-6 over a 256-bit value: the modulo bias (2^256 mod 6) is cryptographically negligible. CAVEAT: this is "csprng-signed" (vrf_method label, vrf.js:18), NOT a true verifiable random function — there is no per-request VRF proof (no input-derived deterministic output, no proving key). Security rests on trusting the signer (now the TEE-attested oracle_verifier) not to grind/select outputs. With the in-TEE flag ON the signer is measured/attested, which is the intended trust anchor; with the flag OFF (current default) randomness is generated by the host-side relayer process before signing, so a compromised host could in principle bias it. Output is bound either way, but unbiasedness depends on the generator's integrity.
lanes.robustness: Strong. Distinct/unbiased draws confirmed empirically on-chain (27/27 distinct, 50.4% bit balance). Idempotency is multi-layered: in-tick seenKeys set [request-processor.js:56], cross-tick hasProcessedEvent+isEventQueuedForRetry [lines 57-58], cross-lane trackedRequestIds/excludedRequestIds membership exclusion [lines 97-100], duplicates_skipped_total metric, AND on-chain the kernel rejects non-Pending requests (EVM RequestNotPending [.sol:157]; N3 "already fulfilled" treated as terminal-success by classifyError [fulfillment.js:66-68]). The dice consumer settle path is itself idempotent (b.status!=Pending guard [.sol:143]) and payouts use a pull-fallback (pendingWithdrawals) so a failed push never reverts settlement [.sol:168-172].
lanes.fault_recovery: Robust. Transient enclave/worker HTTP failures (5xx/429/408/425/0) are classified transient and retried, never finalized as permanent on-chain failures [fulfillment.js:471-478, isTransientWorkerStatus]. Retry uses exponential backoff with jitter [computeRetryDelayMs:173-180]. Once the callback-delivery retry ceiling is exceeded, the event is dead-lettered: recorded 'exhausted' locally, mirrored to the durable Supabase queue (read by /api/relayer/dead-letters for manual replay), metrics incremented, and a push alert fired [recordDeliveryExhaustion:1074-1129]. Ground-truth: retry+dead-letter queues EMPTY (0), checkpoints advancing. App-layer self-healing: MiniAppDiceGameEVM.settleFromKernel [.sol:135-139] lets ANYONE permissionlessly finalize a stuck bet from the kernel's stored signed result if the best-effort callback failed — so a dropped callback does not strand funds. Tests cover transient-retry, ceiling-exhaustion dead-letter, FAULT short-circuit, and finalize-lane re-enqueue (79/79 pass).
lanes.efficiency: Good. Flag-ON path is a single atomic /oracle/fulfill (compute+sign in one enclave round-trip) vs the older two-step (host compute + separate /sign/payload). VRF needs no upstream provider fetch — it is just 32 CSPRNG bytes + one signature, so latency is dominated by the on-chain submit. Concurrency-bounded event processing (mapWithConcurrency), block/request cursor batching (maxBlocksPerTick), and dedup before compute avoid redundant work. The 32-byte randomness rides the compact-callback lane (raw bytes, no JSON envelope) keeping the on-chain result minimal (32B).

issues.desc: Not a true VRF: vrf_method='csprng-signed' (vrf.js:18) — there is no per-request cryptographic VRF proof binding output to a seed/proving-key. Unbiasedness is a trust assumption on the signer, not a verifiable property. Acceptable ONLY because the generator+signer now run in the TEE-attested oracle_verifier; should be documented as 'attested CSPRNG' not 'VRF' to avoid overclaiming.

The in-TEE path is flag-gated and config.js:438 defaults MORPHEUS_RELAYER_ENCLAVE_FULFILL to FALSE. When OFF, VRF randomness is generated by the host relayer process via crypto.randomBytes(32) (fulfillment.js:918) BEFORE signing — a compromised host could bias randomness while still producing a valid signature. The on-chain VRF results were all generated/fulfilled successfully, but could not confirm from public surfaces whether prod runs flag-ON; the digest cross-check and TEE attestation only fully constrain the host when the flag is ON. Recommend explicitly verifying/enforcing flag-ON in production.

Dice consumer reduces randomness via keccak256(result)%6 (MiniAppDiceGameEVM.sol:155); modulo bias over 6 from a 256-bit value is negligible but non-zero. Immaterial in practice; noted for completeness.

One historical VRF request left Pending on Neo N3 mainnet (#1 vrf-e2e, status=0) — appears to be the first end-to-end test request that was superseded; not a stuck-funds case (e2e test app), and later VRF requests fulfilled cleanly. The kernel exposes expireStaleRequest for such cases.

lanes.evidence:
KEY TOPOLOGY (resolved from code + live on-chain/HTTP reads):

(a) WHERE THE BOX SOURCES ITS X25519 KEY — resolution priority in crypto.js ensureOracleKeyMaterial (workers/nitro-worker/src/oracle/crypto.js:600-642):
  1. parseConfiguredOracleKeyMaterial() — reads MORPHEUS/PHALA_ORACLE_KEY_MATERIAL_JSON|BASE64 (crypto.js:61-112). This is the live path: enclave-server.mjs materializeOracleKeyFromKms() (line 1089-1125) KMS-decrypts MORPHEUS_ORACLE_KMS_CIPHERTEXT_BASE64 in-TEE and writes the plaintext {public_key_raw,private_key_pkcs8} JSON onto MORPHEUS_ORACLE_KEY_MATERIAL_JSON. The validated session note ("X25519 key materialized via KMS") = THIS path. So the box decrypts with whatever keypair was KMS-encrypted at provision time.
  2. loadStableOracleKeyMaterial() (crypto.js:217-264) — sealed-keystore fallback: env MORPHEUS_ORACLE_SEALED_KEYSTORE_*, else the disk file /data/morpheus/oracle-key.json (PHALA_ORACLE_KEYSTORE_PATH), unsealed with a wrap key DERIVED deterministically from Secrets Manager master morpheus/x25519-wrap (nitro-signer.js deriveKeyBytes, line 146-167). If neither exists it GENERATES a fresh random X25519 keypair (crypto.js:247-263) and persists it. The keystore's public_key_raw is set ONCE at first generation; the wrap key is deterministic but the keypair is random.
  3. ephemeral in-memory (only if MORPHEUS_ALLOW_EPHEMERAL_KEY).
  The box ALWAYS decrypts with keyMaterial.publicKeyRawBytes as the HKDF salt + info tail (crypto.js deriveAesKey:543-559, decryptX25519Envelope:586-591) and keyMaterial.privateKeyPkcs8Bytes for the X25519 ECDH.

(b) WHY THE THREE KEYS DISAGREE:
  - On-chain publication is a MANUAL, ADMIN-SIGNED step, decoupled from the box's actual key. Contract MorpheusOracle.cs SetRuntimeEncryptionKey/SetOracleEncryptionKey (line 574-592) is ValidateAdmin-gated and bumps key_version. scripts/publish-oracle-public-key.mjs fetches the running box's /oracle/public-key then invokes setOracleEncryptionKey. NOTHING auto-syncs the box's current key on-chain.
  - LIVE on-chain reads (invokefunction): TESTNET kernel 0x4b882e94 oracleEncryptionPublicKey = string "kQvtpPekS1fzHFhaU2XFyKVobY0pii/KrVZSEOD/xx4=" (decodes to 32-byte X25519 point hex 910beda4f7a44b57f31c585a5365c5c8a5686d8d298a2fcaad565210e0ffc71e), key_version=7; MAINNET kernel 0xf54d8584 oracleEncryptionPublicKey = null, version=0 (never published), verifier=03399c42… present.
  - Keystore public_key_raw "PqIQoaPLlaI7XBk/UexmHgPOt+k/XydOl/pZqLKGYwo=" decodes to hex 3ea210a1a3cb95a23b5c193f51ec661e03ceb7e93f5f274e97fa59a8b286630a — DIFFERENT from the on-chain testnet key at the byte level (verified: browser-decoded on-chain bytes != keystore bytes). So the testnet on-chain key (910beda4…, v7) is a STALE/ROTATED publication from a PRIOR testnet enclave generation; the retained disk keystore (3ea210a1…) is yet another, older self-generated key superseded by the KMS-injected key the live box now uses. Three keys = three different lifecycles (admin-published-on-testnet vs disk-keystore vs KMS-materialized), never reconciled.
  - LIVE /health: runtime.meshmini.app AND oracle.meshmini.app both report network:"mainnet", oracle_verifier_public_key:03399c42… (matches MAINNET kernel verifier). The live enclave is MAINNET-configured. There is NO live testnet decrypt box.

(c) CAN A CLIENT ENCRYPT-THEN-DECRYPT END TO END?
  - The binding is strict: browser-encryption.ts (apps/web/lib/browser-encryption.ts:39-86) uses recipientPublicKeyBytes (the on-chain key, decoded) as BOTH the X25519 ECDH recipient AND the HKDF salt+info tail; the box uses ITS OWN publicKeyRawBytes for salt+info and its own private key for ECDH. Round-trip succeeds ONLY if on-chain-key bytes == box-key bytes EXACTLY.
  - PROVEN locally by reproducing both sides with WebCrypto X25519+HKDF+AES-256-GCM: CASE1 (on-chain==box) decrypts; CASE2 (on-chain!=box) throws "OperationError: The operation failed for an operation-specific reason" — the EXACT generic error the user observed. A key mismatch is indistinguishable at the API from a malformed payload.
  - MAINNET: confidential compute is UNUSABLE — on-chain key is null/v0, so apps/web /api/oracle/public-key?network=mainnet returns available:false degraded onchain-unset. A client has no key to encrypt to. (Confirmed live: www.morpheus-matrix.dev/api/oracle/public-key?network=mainnet → available:false.)
  - TESTNET: a client CAN fetch the on-chain key (910beda4…) and encrypt, but (i) there is no live testnet box (runtime is mainnet), and (ii) even routed to a testnet box, decrypt would only succeed if that box still holds the v7 keypair — the disk keystore (3ea210a1…) does NOT match it, so unless the testnet box's KMS/configured key equals 910beda4… the AES-GCM tag fails. Unverifiable as working; topology shows mismatch.
  - The public decrypt lane is not even reachable: oracle.meshmini.app /oracle/decrypt and /oracle/fulfill return 503 runtime_route_unavailable (edge gateway; only /health,/v1/status,/oracle/public-key,/providers,/feeds/* are public). Decrypt only runs via the relayer's authenticated/in-TEE path.

CACHE-POISON GUARD: ensureOracleKeyMaterial (crypto.js:629-638) memoizes the key promise in oracleKeyMaterialPromise but attaches pending.catch() that clears the singleton if the promise REJECTED, so a boot-window failure (before provisioning lands) is retried rather than permanently caching a rejection. Correct. NOTE: this guards a REJECTED resolution only — once a key (even the wrong/stale one) RESOLVES, it is cached for the process lifetime; a silently-mismatched key is NOT self-healing without an enclave restart + re-provision + on-chain re-publish.

GATEWAY BUG (secondary): oracle.meshmini.app /oracle/public-key IGNORES ?network — it returns the TESTNET key (source.network=testnet, contract=0x4b88…) for BOTH ?network=mainnet and ?network=testnet. The canonical apps/web route (www.morpheus-matrix.dev/api) correctly distinguishes (mainnet→null, testnet→kQvt…). A mainnet client trusting oracle.meshmini.app would seal to a testnet key the mainnet box cannot decrypt.
apps_using: forever-album (encrypted blob storage — confidential storage lane)
any miniapp posting encrypted_payload/encrypted_inputs through compute/index.js or oracle/fetch.js (confidential API-token + confidential compute lanes)
lanes.correctness: The crypto IMPLEMENTATION is correct and symmetric (client browser-encryption.ts and box crypto.js use identical algorithm: X25519 ECDH → HKDF-SHA256 with salt=recipientPub, info='morpheus-confidential-payload-v2'||epk||recipientPub → AES-256-GCM; envelope v2 fields epk/iv/ct/tag validated; IV=12B, tag=16B enforced). Local round-trip reproduction confirms the algorithm decrypts correctly when keys match. The DEFECT is OPERATIONAL/topological, not algorithmic: the on-chain published key is decoupled from the box's actual keypair via a manual admin publish step, and the three observed keys (on-chain testnet 910beda4… v7, disk keystore 3ea210a1…, live KMS-materialized) are mutually inconsistent. MAINNET has no key at all (null/v0) so the lane is dead there. The strict HKDF salt/info binding to the recipient public key means ANY mismatch silently fails decryption with a generic OperationError.
lanes.robustness: Mixed. Strong points: in-TEE KMS key materialization (host never sees plaintext), deterministic wrap-key derivation from Secrets Manager, cache-poison guard against caching a REJECTED key resolution, AES-GCM tamper-evidence, strict envelope structural validation, recipient-binding HKDF (prevents key-confusion/cross-recipient replay), encrypted-ref requester/callback scoping + single-use _consumed_request_id claim (crypto.js:385-500). Weak points: (1) no invariant tying the box's live public_key_raw to the on-chain published key — drift is silent and only surfaces as opaque decrypt failure; (2) once a (possibly wrong) key resolves it is cached process-wide with no re-validation; (3) failures are indistinguishable (mismatched key, tampered ciphertext, and malformed payload can all surface as the same generic error); (4) gateway oracle.meshmini.app serves the wrong-network key.
lanes.fault_recovery: Boot-window resilience is good: ensureOracleKeyMaterial clears the memoized promise on rejection (crypto.js:635-637) so an encrypted request arriving before provision-enclave-compute.sh injects the key does not permanently poison the lane. The provisioning path is reversible (remove the KMS ciphertext file → fall back to host-unseal plaintext path, provision-enclave-compute.sh:96-119). HOWEVER recovery from a KEY-TOPOLOGY drift is NOT automatic: it requires operator action — restart/re-provision the enclave with the intended keypair AND re-run scripts/publish-oracle-public-key.mjs (admin-signed setOracleEncryptionKey, version bump) so the on-chain key matches the box. For mainnet the key has simply never been published (Phase C task #35 still in_progress).
lanes.efficiency: Not a concern for this lane. Per-request crypto is a single X25519 ECDH + one HKDF + one AES-GCM decrypt (sub-millisecond); key material is resolved once and memoized. The Supabase encrypted-ref lookup adds a bounded (15s timeout) network round-trip + a single-use PATCH claim only when encrypted_*_ref is used (not for inline encrypted_payload).

issues.desc: MAINNET confidential compute is non-functional: mainnet kernel 0xf54d8584 oracleEncryptionPublicKey is null (version 0) — never published. apps/web returns available:false degraded for ?network=mainnet, so a client has NO key to encrypt to. Any mainnet app on the confidential lane (private-transfer, neo-message time-lock, neodid, forever-album) cannot perform an end-to-end confidential request. Fix: run publish-oracle-public-key.mjs against the live mainnet box's actual /oracle/public-key (Phase C task #35).

Key topology is inconsistent across all three sources with no reconciliation: on-chain testnet key (910beda4…, v7) != disk keystore key (3ea210a1… / PqIQ…) != live KMS-materialized key. The on-chain testnet publication is stale (prior enclave generation). Because client and box both bind HKDF salt+info to the recipient public key, a mismatch silently fails decryption (proven: produces the exact 'OperationError / operation failed for an operation-specific reason'). On testnet, even if routed to a box, decrypt will fail unless the box's current key byte-equals 910beda4….

oracle.meshmini.app /oracle/public-key ignores the ?network query param and ALWAYS returns the TESTNET key (source.network=testnet, contract=0x4b88…) — including for ?network=mainnet. A mainnet client trusting this gateway would seal to a key the mainnet box cannot decrypt. The canonical apps/web route (www.morpheus-matrix.dev/api/oracle/public-key) is correct; clients must use it, not the edge gateway.

No invariant/test binds the box's live public_key_raw to the on-chain oracleEncryptionPublicKey. crypto.test.mjs only asserts the unseal source label, never that the materialized public key matches what is published on-chain. Any key regeneration (fresh enclave with empty keystore generates a NEW random keypair, crypto.js:247), KMS re-provision with a different key, or network switch silently breaks the lane until an operator manually re-publishes. Recommend a startup/health assertion: box.public_key_raw == on-chain oracleEncryptionPublicKey for the configured network, surfaced in /health and fulfillment-readiness.

Decrypt failure modes are indistinguishable: a stale-key mismatch, a tampered ciphertext, and a structurally-valid-but-wrong-recipient envelope all surface as the same generic AES-GCM OperationError, while a non-v2/non-GCM payload surfaces 'unsupported confidential payload format'. Operators cannot tell a key-topology bug from a client error without out-of-band info. Consider a key_version/recipient-fingerprint check in the envelope so a mismatch is reported explicitly.

Once a key resolves (even a stale/wrong one), ensureOracleKeyMaterial caches it for the process lifetime; the cache-poison guard only clears on REJECTION, not on a resolved-but-mismatched key. A silently-wrong key is not self-healing without an enclave restart.

lanes.evidence:
PRICE FEEDS LIVE-VERIFIED ON-CHAIN (2026-06-15):
- Neo N3 mainnet datafeed 0x03013f49c42a14546c8bbe58f9d434c3517fccab getLatest: NEO-USD=2.323, GAS-USD=1.162, BTC-USD=66722.51, AAPL-USD=292.12, SPY-USD=752.51; all recordTs=1781530310 (2026-06-15T13:31:50Z, within the hour). getAllPairs returns 36 pairs incl. the 10 restored equities/ETFs (AAPL/GOOGL/MSFT/AMZN/TSLA/META/NVDA/SPY/QQQ/GLD) + commodities (WTI/BRENT/COPPER/WHEAT). 6-decimal scale (DECIMALS=6).
- Neo X mainnet MorpheusPriceFeed 0x38DD6BCE… getLatest("TWELVEDATA:NEO-USD")=2.328, ts=1781530421 (2026-06-15T13:33:41Z, fresh); bare "NEO-USD" returns exists=0 (must use TWELVEDATA: prefix). selector keccak256(getLatest(string))=0xd24678df. Both chains write the same 1e6-scaled integer; NEO ≈ $2.32 consistent across N3+NeoX.
- Public API oracle.meshmini.app/feeds/price/NEO-USD = 2.321, ts 2026-06-15T13:42:01Z, source:onchain, contract 0x9bea75cf (testnet). runtime.meshmini.app/health: ready, compute+signer ok, oracle_verifier 03399c42….

SMART-FETCH/QUERY PATH: router.js:248-254 maps oracle.query/oracle.smart_fetch→moduleId oracle.fetch→/oracle/smart-fetch. fetch.js performOracleFetch: named-provider lane (symbol→twelvedata/binance/coinbase) vs arbitrary-URL lane (payload.url). enclave-server.mjs:430-500 isHostUnattestedLane: arbitrary-URL is tagged host-unattested + NOT signed in-TEE; symbol/provider lane computed+signed in-enclave (TRUST_TIER_ENCLAVE_ATTESTED). Execution-plane passthrough (enclave-server.mjs:1397-1414) serves /oracle/query + /oracle/smart-fetch in-TEE via computeViaWorker→127.0.0.1.

OPEN FINDING (confidential-key mismatch) RESOLVED FROM CODE+CHAIN: authoritative X25519 key = KMS-attested/configured key, NOT the keystore PqIQ… value. boot calls materializeOracleKeyFromKms() (enclave-server.mjs:1089-1125) → sets MORPHEUS_ORACLE_KEY_MATERIAL_JSON; crypto.js ensureOracleKeyMaterial() loads parseConfiguredOracleKeyMaterial FIRST (priority), loadStableOracleKeyMaterial (keystore PqIQ…) only as fallback. provision-enclave-compute.sh:98-119 prefers KMS ciphertext over host-unseal. On-chain verified: testnet kernel 0x4b882e94 oracleEncryptionPublicKey decodes to kQvt…=EOD/xx4 (version 7) = exactly what /oracle/public-key serves (key_source neo_n3_contract) = what clients seal to → testnet decrypt coherent. MAINNET kernel 0xf54d8584 oracleEncryptionPublicKey=null, version 0 → mainnet confidential lane has NO published key.
lanes.correctness: Correct. Price-feed read path is sound: useMorpheusDataFeed (shared/composables) reads getLatest via JSON-RPC invokefunction, descales by fixed 1e6, returns dataTimestamp + recordTimestamp so callers can detect freshness; FAULT/non-HALT and wrong-shape are thrown, not silently zeroed (useMorpheusDataFeed.ts:136-159). Fulfillment digest binding is correct and consensus-safe: buildFulfillmentDigestBytes binds domain+requestId+sha256(appId/moduleId/operation)+success+sha256(result)+sha256(error)+deployment(scriptHash+networkMagic) (crypto/router.js:302-375); relayer recomputes the digest locally and asserts byte-equality vs the enclave-returned fulfillment_digest_hex before submit (fulfillment.js:510-518) — catches enclave/relayer drift. Aggregation (aggregation.js) does median + outlier rejection (maxDeviationPct=25) + trimmed-mean + confidence labels with correct 1/2/3+ source handling. Confidential X25519-HKDF-SHA256-AES-256-GCM envelope (info morpheus-confidential-payload-v2, v2) is implemented symmetrically; the testnet on-chain published key matches the KMS-materialized in-TEE key.
lanes.robustness: Strong. Providers layer (providers.js): per-provider CircuitBreaker, response cache with in-flight dedup (singleflight), retry with exponential backoff honoring Retry-After, body-size caps (64KB provider / 256KB upstream), capped LRU caches. fetch.js: comprehensive SSRF guard (DNS-resolve all addrs + IPv4/IPv6/NAT64/v4-mapped private-range checks, redirect:error to stop rebinding, re-validate host immediately before connect), typed UpstreamFetchError (502/504 vs 400 caller-fixable). Feed submission policy (sync-policy.js shouldSubmitFeed): Chainlink-style deviation threshold (thresholdBps) + heartbeat stale-refresh (staleAfterMs) + min-update-interval rate-limit. clampFeedTimestampSec enforces strict monotonicity and rejects poisoned far-future upstream timestamps (skips the pair instead of stalling it). On-chain contracts enforce monotonic timestamp / StaleRound (Neo X MorpheusPriceFeed.sol:33). Apps fail safe: neo-swap canSwap returns false when rateStale OR router unavailable (useSwapEngine.ts:174-176, RATE_STALE_AFTER_MS=10m keyed on recordTimestamp); neo-treasury renders warning + suppresses USD when totalUsd==null (PlayArea.tsx:97,257).
lanes.fault_recovery: Good. Relayer queue.js: durable retry queue with next_retry_at backoff, dead-letter queue with limits, manual replay/finalize-only, transient-vs-terminal classification (isTransientWorkerStatus 5xx/429/408/425/0 → throw transient error to retry, never finalize a permanent failure that would strand a request). Validated live: fulfill_success ~29, retry+dead-letter queues EMPTY. Provider circuit breakers degrade gracefully; multi-provider feeds aggregate around failed sources (errors[] collected, surviving quotes aggregated). On-chain baseline-load failure with no local records returns 503 (does not anchor blind). crypto.js ensureOracleKeyMaterial does NOT cache a rejected key resolution (clears singleton on failure) so a decrypt request during the enclave boot window before KMS provisioning lands does not permanently poison the lane. confidential.decrypt transient enclave failure is retried, not finalized as a permanent decrypt-failed callback (fulfillment.js:949-953).
lanes.efficiency: Good. Provider response cache (TTL) + in-flight singleflight dedup avoids duplicate upstream calls; capped LRU prevents unbounded growth. Client-side getPrices() has a 5-min in-memory cache (shared/utils/price.ts). On-chain reads are single invokefunction calls (~300ms, no GAS). Feed batching: handleOracleFeed batches all symbol updates into one MorpheusDataFeed batchUpdate tx and only submits pairs that pass the deviation/heartbeat policy (minimizes on-chain writes/GAS). fast-ack path (202 + background task) for feed sync. apps/web public-key + feeds routes set cache-control max-age. Minor: aggregation deviation guard is computed but the on-chain feed value is the per-provider quote (each provider→own storage_pair record), so a single-provider pair (default twelvedata) gets no cross-source deviation protection on the anchored value — the median/outlier rejection is informational only for single-source feeds.

issues.desc: MAINNET confidential lane is unusable: Neo N3 mainnet kernel 0xf54d8584 oracleEncryptionPublicKey is null / oracleEncryptionKeyVersion=0 (verified on-chain). /oracle/public-key correctly degrades to available:false,degraded:true for mainnet, so mainnet clients cannot seal an X25519 envelope and confidential decrypt/private-transfer/neo-message reveal cannot be initiated on mainnet. The in-TEE key exists (KMS-materialized) but its public half was never published to the mainnet kernel. Fix = publish the enclave's X25519 public key to the mainnet kernel oracleEncryptionPublicKey (the testnet kernel already has it, kQvt…, version 7).

Public oracle data routes (/prices, /oracle/query, /oracle/smart-fetch, /oracle/decrypt) return runtime_route_unavailable ('restoration in progress') on oracle.meshmini.app, and the runtime gateway returns 404 for the public unauth path. Only /health, /v1/status, /oracle/public-key, /providers, /feeds/catalog, /feeds/price/<symbol> are publicly reachable. The on-chain feeds (the price-consumption lane apps actually use) are fully fresh and read directly from chain, so app price reads are unaffected — but the public off-chain fetch/compute/decrypt API surface is not yet restored end-to-end, so a live decrypt round-trip could not be exercised externally (read-only scope, no box-private routes).
MEMORY note 'testnet datafeed 0x9bea75cf frozen 2026-03-09' is OUTDATED — that feed is now fresh (recordTs 2026-06-15T13:40:01Z, verified). Stale-feed concern for testnet no longer applies.

For single-provider feeds (default twelvedata for most pairs), aggregateQuotes deviation/outlier rejection is informational only (returns single-source); the on-chain anchored value has no cross-source sanity check. Adding a second provider per critical pair (e.g. binance/coinbase for NEO/GAS/BTC) would let the median+25%-deviation guard actually gate the anchored price.

lanes.evidence: Read the full relayer pipeline: fulfillment.js (1797 lines), request-processor.js, relayer.js, state.js, queue.js, config.js, nitro.js, neo-n3.js, neox.js. Ran the recovery-path test suites: 155/155 pass (fulfillment.test.mjs 73, request-processor.test.mjs 4, queue.test.mjs 39, state.test.mjs 10, neox.test.mjs 23) including atomic-state-write, crash-mid-write, jitter-desync, B2 reconciliation-picks-up-missed-request, retry-queue-overflow-shedding, isDurableQueueReadyJob. Live on-chain confirms both kernels advancing: Neo N3 mainnet 0xf54d8584 getTotalRequests=7 (HALT, via mainnet1.neo.coz.io); Neo X mainnet 0xeCFC1C65 totalRequests=0x1e=30 with oracleVerifier=0x622ae03bdb6d7e2a29be853c75d625bb25c0139c (via mainnet-1.rpc.banelabs.org); oracle.meshmini.app/mainnet/v1/status runtime=operational. Cited metrics (fulfill_success 29, fulfill_failure 2, dead_letter 0, reconciliation_failures 0, retries advancing) are consistent with the code's metric increments — not independently re-read (relayer /metrics is auth-gated on the public surface, only trustless on-chain + /v1/status routes are public, by design).
apps_using: workers/morpheus-relayer (Neo N3 + Neo X fulfillment, both mainnet kernels)
all oracle-consuming miniapps (dice-game/VRF, neo-message confidential decrypt, automation-copilot, price-feed consumers) via the shared fulfillment pipeline
lanes.correctness: Retry/backoff is correct: exponential 2^(attempts-1) capped at retryMaxDelayMs with full/equal jitter [0.5,1.0]*ceiling (fulfillment.js:173-184, state.js:420-428) to prevent a shared-dependency-recovery stampede — verified by the 'desynchronizes two equal-attempt retries' test. Error classification (fulfillment.js:142-171) correctly retries transient HTTP (0/408/425/429/5xx) and never burns them into a permanent on-chain failure callback, while terminal config errors (unauthorized/invalid-sig/verifier-rejected) fast-fail. Idempotency: already-fulfilled → 'settled' terminal (fulfillment.js:1610); Neo N3/Neo X RequestNotPending both normalize to 'already fulfilled' (neox.js:391); filterNewEvents + collectActiveRequestIds dedupe cross-lane/cross-tick; confirmNeoN3FulfillExecution throws-on-timeout so a broadcast-but-never-mined tx is re-broadcast (idempotent) rather than silently recorded fulfilled (neo-n3.js:983-1018). Dual-cursor (block + request) with confirmation depth (neo_n3:1, neox:2). Digest cross-check before submit in enclave-fulfill path (fulfillment.js:509-520). Digests bind contract+networkMagic/chainId so signatures can't replay across deployments.
lanes.robustness: Strong. Reconciliation is scan-by-request-id (request-processor.js:70-120), NOT event-subscription-dependent — a missed request not in excludedRequestIds∪collectActiveRequestIds is still reconciled (B2 test). Discovery is decoupled from retry-drain: a discovery-RPC throw logs discovery_failures_total and falls through to runDueRetries WITHOUT advancing the cursor (request-processor.js:159-204, 264-303); reconciliation is in its own try/catch so it can't crash a tick after retries ran. Atomic state persistence via temp-file+rename (state.js:135-147) — crash-mid-write cannot truncate state (tested). Per-candidate RPC failover: Neo N3 rotates the rpcUrls list on transport error and promotes the healthy URL (neo-n3.js:68-115); Neo X withNeoXReadFailover fails over only on transport errors, throwing deterministic CALL_EXCEPTION/revert immediately (neox.js:139-155). Backpressure: maxFreshEventsPerTick(32)/maxRetryEventsPerTick(16) caps + retryQueueLimit shedding to recoverable dead-letters (queue.js:86-136, state.js:298-329).
lanes.fault_recovery: Comprehensive. Hung RPC/origin fails fast: AbortController timeouts everywhere (Nitro 10s nitro.js:64-67, Neo RPC 30s neo-n3.js:36/77, n3index 30s, Neo X receipt 45s via Promise.race that also covers a wait() that never settles, neox.js:414-431). Neo X submit is serialized per-signer (runExclusive) with signer.reset() nonce re-sync on failure so a stuck/replaced tx can't wedge the queue or the whole loop (neox.js:452-492). Dead-letter: callback-ceiling/permanent → recordDeliveryExhaustion pushes to dead_letters + Supabase 'exhausted' for manual replay + fires a dedicated dead-letter heartbeat (fulfillment.js:1074-1157). Durable cross-instance claim (queue.js:313-409): conflict drops+counts, backoff retains the item (never cleared, never counted as conflict), stale-processing reclaim via staleProcessingMs. Loop-level catch in runRelayerLoop sends a failure heartbeat and sleeps rather than dying (relayer.js:254-262). Manual retry/replay lane re-injects dead-lettered jobs (queue.js:138-198).

issues.desc: allowLocalClaimDuringBackoff defaults true (config.js:339): during a Supabase outage a hypothetical SECOND relayer instance could double-broadcast the same fulfillment because the cross-instance claim is offline. Mitigated: current topology is single-instance, the warning is logged, and the on-chain RequestNotPending/already-fulfilled revert is the definitive backstop (a double-broadcast just FAULTs as already-fulfilled). Multi-instance deploys must set it false.

retryQueueLimit defaults to 0 = UNBOUNDED (config.js:330). A sustained ingestion burst + downstream outage can grow the in-memory retry_queue without ceiling, and the whole array re-serializes on every persist (a memory + IO amplification SPOF on the single relayer box). The shedding-to-dead-letter safety only engages once an operator opts in by setting MORPHEUS_RELAYER_RETRY_QUEUE_LIMIT; the default live box has no hard cap.

No global circuit breaker / adaptive overload-shed: recovery from a sustained dependency outage relies on per-tick caps + backoff + retry-queue limit rather than tripping a breaker to stop hammering. Acceptable for the current low-volume topology (cited ~29 fulfillments) but would not scale-protect under a request storm coincident with an RPC/Nitro outage.

Relayer /metrics (the source of the cited fulfill_success/dead_letter/reconciliation_failures gauges) is not exposed on the public trustless surface (oracle.meshmini.app returns runtime_route_unavailable for /v1/relayer; apps/web /api/relayer/metrics returns unauthorized), so the cited live metric values could not be independently re-verified in this read-only probe — only the on-chain checkpoints (kernel request counts advancing) and runtime /v1/status were directly confirmed.

verdicts.reasoning:
I re-ran every cited on-chain read and re-read the cited code. The randomness/binding/fulfillment claims hold; what I cannot independently confirm is whether production runs the randomness generation inside the TEE.

CONFIRMED INDEPENDENTLY (live, today):
- Neo X mainnet kernel 0xeCFC1C652B5cCdBfe3E9314a83156787D92a3fD2 @block 6817683: totalRequests=30; oracleVerifier==updater==0x622ae03BDB6d7E2A29BE853c75d625bB25c0139C. Enumerated all 30 getRequest(): 27 random.generate ALL status=Succeeded (0 Pending, 0 Failed), every result 32 bytes, 27/27 DISTINCT, bit balance 50.39% ones over 6912 bits (matches note 50.4%) => unbiased CSPRNG. By appId: vrf-e2e 7, dice-evm 5, dice 15 (=20 dice). Recent fulfillment latency 12-45s (req->fulfilled), last on 2026-06-07.
- Neo N3 mainnet kernel 0xf54d8584...: getTotalRequests=7, getTotalFulfilled=6. VRF #2-5 Succeeded with 4 distinct 32B results (c0d552ee/aacc53bf/2dbb64a7/f6c13506); #1 left Pending (first test req); #7 compute.run is Failed (not VRF).
- Code at every cited line: vrf.js:6 crypto.getRandomValues(32); fulfillment.js:917-918 host-local crypto.randomBytes(32) when flag OFF; config.js:438 enclaveFulfill defaults FALSE; fulfillment.js:509-520 digest cross-check refuses to submit on mismatch; neox.js:332-364 buildNeoXDigest byte-identical to MorpheusOracleEVM.fulfillmentDigest (.sol:144-151) then EIP-191 sign (neox.js:372); MorpheusOracleEVM.fulfillRequest re-derives digest + ecrecover==oracleVerifier else BadSignature (.sol:159-161); MiniAppDiceGameEVM derives die keccak256(result)%6+1 (.sol:155) and settleFromKernel (.sol:135-139) is the permissionless recovery; enclave /oracle/fulfill computes VRF in-process via loopback handler (enclave-server.mjs:331-352,442-453) i.e. genuine in-enclave generation when the flag is ON.
- Tests: vrf.test.mjs+enclave-server.test.mjs 31/31 pass; fulfillment.test.mjs 79/79 pass, including the exact 'flag-ON VRF uses the enclave (no relayer-local randomBytes)', 'flag-OFF keeps two-step host path', and digest cross-check + tamper-rejection for neo_n3/legacy/neox.
- Live enclave: oracle.meshmini.app/health => mainnet morpheus-enclave-server, ready, signer+compute true, oracle_verifier_public_key 03399c42... (matches note ground truth); /v1/status runtime operational, executionPlane tee_runtime.

ADVERSARIAL DOUBT RESOLVED: the verifier pubkeys 03399c42... (/health) and 02911ea2... (/oracle/public-key, N3 testnet) initially failed secp256k1 decompression ('x not on curve'). This was MY wrong-curve test: Neo N3 signs with secp256r1 (P-256) (enclave-server.mjs:361-373 uses neon-js wallet.sign), and both keys ARE valid P-256 points (verified with P-256 params). So the note's '03399c42 matches oracle_verifier ground truth' is correct; not a defect. The Neo X k1 verifier address 0x622ae03b is on-chain and the chain's 27/27 successful fulfillRequest calls PROVE the relayer signatures recover to it (ecrecover==oracleVerifier or revert).

WHY PARTIAL not YES: (1) the lane is 'csprng-signed', NOT a true VRF (vrf.js:18 vrf_method='csprng-signed') — no per-request cryptographic proof; unbiasedness is a trust assumption on the signer, not a verifiable property. (2) The in-TEE generation is flag-gated and DEFAULTS OFF (config.js:438). On-chain success and the empirical distinctness/balance prove the randomness is good and consensus-bound, but they do NOT distinguish enclave-generated from host-generated randomness; a compromised host with the flag OFF could bias randomness while still producing a chain-valid signature. /health shows the enclave ready and session ground-truth asserts 'signs in-TEE', but the actual prod value of MORPHEUS_RELAYER_ENCLAVE_FULFILL is not provable from public surfaces. So randomness generation works and is bound; the TEE trust anchor in production is asserted but not publicly verifiable.
real_issues: Not a true VRF: vrf_method='csprng-signed' (workers/nitro-worker/src/oracle/vrf.js:18) — no per-request VRF proof. Output is signed/consensus-bound but unbiasedness is a trust assumption on the signer, not a cryptographically verifiable property. Should be documented as 'attested CSPRNG', not 'VRF', to avoid overclaiming.

In-TEE randomness is flag-gated and config.js:438 defaults MORPHEUS_RELAYER_ENCLAVE_FULFILL to FALSE. With the flag OFF, VRF randomness is generated by the host relayer process (fulfillment.js:918 crypto.randomBytes(32)) BEFORE signing, so a compromised host could bias randomness while still producing a chain-valid signature. The on-chain results were all valid, but neither the chain nor any public surface proves prod runs flag-ON. Recommend explicitly enforcing/verifying flag-ON in production.
Dice consumer reduces 256-bit randomness via keccak256(result)%6 (MiniAppDiceGameEVM.sol:155). Modulo bias over 6 is cryptographically negligible but non-zero. Immaterial in practice.
One historical VRF request left Pending on Neo N3 mainnet (#1 vrf-e2e, status=0) — the first e2e test request; not stuck funds; kernel exposes expireStaleRequest. Cosmetic.

VRF lane has no live traffic since 2026-06-07 (last Neo X dice request #28). It is request-driven (not a continuously published feed), so this is not a freshness bug, but the lane is only demo/test-exercised, not under sustained production load.

verdicts.reasoning:
Adversarially re-verified every load-bearing claim against the cited code and live reads; the lane's works:"no" verdict is CORRECT and accurately evidenced.

CRYPTO IS CORRECT, OPERATIONALLY BROKEN. Re-read crypto.js (box) and browser-encryption.ts (client): the algorithm is symmetric and sound — X25519 ECDH -> HKDF-SHA256 with salt=recipientPublicKey and info="morpheus-confidential-payload-v2"||epk||recipientPublicKey -> AES-256-GCM; envelope v2 (epk/iv/ct/tag) structurally validated (IV=12B crypto.js:569, tag=16B crypto.js:574). The binding is STRICT: client uses the on-chain key it fetched as recipient for ECDH+salt+info (browser-encryption.ts:39-86); box uses keyMaterial.publicKeyRawBytes for salt+info and its own private key (crypto.js:533-591). Decrypt succeeds ONLY when on-chain-key bytes == box-key bytes exactly. So a key mismatch silently fails with a generic AES-GCM OperationError — the lane's local reproduction of the exact user-observed error is consistent with the code.

KEY MATERIALIZATION verified: enclave-server.mjs:1089 materializeOracleKeyFromKms() KMS-decrypts MORPHEUS_ORACLE_KMS_CIPHERTEXT_BASE64 in-TEE and sets MORPHEUS_ORACLE_KEY_MATERIAL_JSON, which parseConfiguredOracleKeyMaterial() reads FIRST in ensureOracleKeyMaterial (crypto.js:600-642). The on-chain publish (MorpheusOracle.cs:589 SetOracleEncryptionKey -> SetRuntimeEncryptionKey:574, ValidateAdmin-gated, version-bumping) is a MANUAL admin step decoupled from the box's actual key. Nothing auto-syncs them. Phase C task #35 (enclave key sealing / no host-resident keys) is still in_progress, corroborating the unpublished-mainnet state.

LIVE ON-CHAIN + HTTP REINVERIFIED (independent):
- MAINNET kernel 0xf54d8584 oracleEncryptionPublicKey = Any (null), runtimeEncryptionKeyVersion = 0. NEVER published. www.morpheus-matrix.dev/api/oracle/public-key?network=mainnet -> available:false, degraded:true, key_version:0, key_source:"onchain-unset". Mainnet confidential lane is UNUSABLE — client has no key to seal to.
- TESTNET kernel 0x4b882e94 oracleEncryptionPublicKey on-chain stores the base64 STRING "kQvtpPekS1fzHFhaU2XFyKVobY0pii/KrVZSEOD/xx4=" which decodes to X25519 point hex 910beda4f7a44b57f31c585a5365c5c8a5686d8d298a2fcaad565210e0ffc71e (32B). The disk keystore public_key_raw "PqIQ..." decodes to 3ea210a1a3cb95a23b5c193f51ec661e03ceb7e93f5f274e97fa59a8b286630a (32B). I byte-compared them: NOT EQUAL. Three mutually-inconsistent keys (on-chain testnet v7, disk keystore, live KMS-materialized) with no reconciliation — exactly as the lane states.
- Live /health (runtime.meshmini.app) network:mainnet, verifier 03399c42...c395 (matches mainnet kernel verifier per apps/web). No live testnet decrypt box.
- GATEWAY BUG confirmed live and is real: oracle.meshmini.app/oracle/public-key?network=mainnet returns available:true with the TESTNET key kQvt... and source.network:testnet, contract 0x4b88..., verifier ...95cb4. It IGNORES ?network entirely (testnet response for both queries; even its 503s report network:testnet). A mainnet client trusting the edge gateway seals to a key the mainnet box cannot decrypt. The canonical apps/web route is correct (mainnet->available:false).
- Public decrypt unreachable: oracle.meshmini.app /oracle/decrypt and /oracle/fulfill both HTTP 503 runtime_route_unavailable. Decrypt runs only via relayer in-TEE path.

CACHE-POISON GUARD confirmed exactly as described (crypto.js:635-637): clears the memoized promise on REJECTION only; a resolved-but-wrong/stale key is cached process-wide and is NOT self-healing without enclave restart + re-provision + on-chain re-publish.

NET: The crypto is right; the lane is non-functional in practice. Mainnet has no key at all; testnet's on-chain key, disk keystore, and live KMS key disagree and there's no live testnet box to even route to; and the edge gateway hands mainnet clients a testnet key. works=no is the corrected, accurate verdict.
real_issues: CRITICAL: Mainnet confidential compute is dead. Kernel 0xf54d8584 oracleEncryptionPublicKey = null, version 0 (verified live on-chain + via www.morpheus-matrix.dev/api/oracle/public-key?network=mainnet -> available:false, key_source:onchain-unset). Mainnet apps on the confidential lane (private-transfer, neo-message time-lock, neodid, forever-album) cannot do an end-to-end confidential request — a client has no key to encrypt to. Fix = run scripts/publish-oracle-public-key.mjs against the live mainnet box's /oracle/public-key (Phase C task #35, still in_progress).

CRITICAL: Key topology is inconsistent with no reconciliation. On-chain testnet point 910beda4... (v7) != disk keystore point 3ea210a1... (byte-verified) != live KMS-materialized key. Because client and box both bind HKDF salt+info to the recipient public key (crypto.js:543-559 / browser-encryption.ts:51-67), any mismatch silently fails AES-GCM with the generic 'operation failed for an operation-specific reason' the user saw. Even routed to a testnet box, decrypt fails unless that box's current key byte-equals 910beda4...; unverifiable as working and topology shows mismatch.

HIGH: oracle.meshmini.app/oracle/public-key ignores the ?network param and ALWAYS returns the TESTNET key (source.network:testnet, contract 0x4b88...) — including for ?network=mainnet (confirmed live). A mainnet client trusting the edge gateway seals to a key the mainnet box cannot decrypt. Clients must use the canonical www.morpheus-matrix.dev/api/oracle/public-key route (which correctly degrades mainnet to available:false). Even the gateway's 503 errors report network:testnet, confirming it is fully testnet-scoped.

HIGH: No invariant/test binds the box's live public_key_raw to the on-chain oracleEncryptionPublicKey. crypto.test.mjs only asserts the unseal source label, never that the materialized public key matches what's published on-chain. A fresh enclave with empty keystore generates a NEW random keypair (crypto.js:247-263); KMS re-provision with a different key or a network switch silently breaks decryption until an operator manually re-publishes. Recommend a startup/health assertion box.public_key_raw == on-chain key for the configured network, surfaced in /health.

MEDIUM: Decrypt failure modes are indistinguishable — stale-key mismatch, tampered ciphertext, and wrong-recipient envelope all surface as the same generic AES-GCM OperationError, while a non-v2 payload yields 'unsupported confidential payload format'. Operators cannot distinguish a key-topology bug from a client error without out-of-band info. Consider a key_version/recipient-fingerprint field in the envelope so a mismatch is reported explicitly.

LOW: Once a key resolves (even a stale/wrong one), ensureOracleKeyMaterial caches it for the process lifetime (crypto.js:629-639); the cache-poison guard clears only on REJECTION (line 635-637), not on a resolved-but-mismatched key. A silently-wrong key is not self-healing without an enclave restart + re-provision + on-chain re-publish.

INFO: Public /oracle/decrypt and /oracle/fulfill return HTTP 503 runtime_route_unavailable — the confidential decrypt lane is not directly reachable from the public edge; it executes only via the relayer's authenticated in-TEE path. This is by design but means the lane cannot be black-box validated end-to-end from outside, and mainnet cannot be validated at all until the key is published.

verdicts.reasoning:
CORRECTED VERDICT: works=partial, not yes. The PRICE-DATA core of this lane is genuinely live and fresh — I re-ran every on-chain read. Neo N3 mainnet datafeed 0x03013f49c42a14546c8bbe58f9d434c3517fccab getLatest returns fresh prices at 6-decimal scale: TWELVEDATA:NEO-USD=2.323 (ts 2026-06-15T13:31:50Z, 18min old), GAS=1.162, BTC=66513.2, AAPL=295.39, SPY=753.34; getAllPairs returns 36 pairs incl. the 10 equities/ETFs + commodities. Neo X mainnet 0x38DD6BCE… getLatest("TWELVEDATA:NEO-USD")=2.321 exists=1 (8min old). Public API oracle.meshmini.app/feeds/price/NEO-USD=2.321 source:onchain. runtime.meshmini.app/health ready, oracle_verifier 03399c42…. The frontend read path (neo-miniapps-platform/apps/shared/composables/useMorpheusDataFeed.ts:130-162) is sound: asserts HALT, validates struct shape (throws, never silently zeroes), normalizeAsset() prepends TWELVEDATA: (lines 73-79), fixed 1e6 descale. So price-consumption apps read fresh, correct data.

BUT the validation overstated three things the system prompt told me to verify skeptically:

(1) EVIDENCE ERROR: validation implied bare getLatest("NEO-USD") on the N3 datafeed works (NEO=2.323). It does NOT — bare returns a ZERO/empty struct {price:0, ts:0}; the TWELVEDATA: prefix is mandatory on BOTH chains. App code prefixes correctly so apps are unaffected, but the cited evidence is wrong.

(2) VRF NOT CONFIRMED IN-TEE (contradicts the lane's stated ground truth). workers/morpheus-relayer/src/fulfillment.js:917-918 generates VRF randomness with relayer-local crypto.randomBytes(32). It is gated by config.nitro.enclaveFulfill (config.js:438, DEFAULT FALSE), and config.js:431-437 explicitly states "The live box runs flag-off." enclave-server.mjs:438-440 confirms "the relayer today short-circuits with crypto.randomBytes." In-TEE signing (the /sign/payload step) is real, but signing ≠ randomness generation. Unless the cutover flag is ON in prod (unverifiable from read-only), VRF randomness is produced OUTSIDE the TEE.

(3) CONFIDENTIAL DECRYPT NOT VERIFIABLE END-TO-END. Public /oracle/decrypt returns 503 runtime_route_unavailable; runtime gateway 404s the unauth path; /oracle/query=401. No round-trip is possible. The validation's claim that the KMS-materialized enclave key == on-chain kQvt key is CIRCULAR/unverified: the public-key endpoint sources its key FROM the on-chain testnet contract (apps/web/app/api/oracle/public-key/route.ts:38-51, key_source neo_n3_contract), NOT from the enclave's actual key material (crypto.js ensureOracleKeyMaterial loads MORPHEUS_ORACLE_KEY_MATERIAL_JSON whose public_key_raw was never compared on-chain). Three distinct keys exist: keystore PqIQ…, on-chain testnet kQvt…, and the KMS enclave key (public half unverified). Whether the enclave private key actually decrypts envelopes sealed to kQvt is an assumption.

Mainnet confidential lane is genuinely dead (validation's HIGH issue is correct): mainnet kernel 0xf54d8584 oracleEncryptionPublicKey = {"type":"Any"} (null, verified on-chain); testnet 0x4b882e94 = kQvt… (decoded, matches endpoint). On-chain fulfillment IS happening (testnet requests 6100-6119 all fulfilled=true with result bytes), but newest fulfilled testnet request is ~15 days old and mainnet kernel has only 7 lifetime requests — low/no recent demand. Net: price-feed sub-lane works (yes); VRF-in-TEE and confidential-decrypt sub-lanes are not confirmed working → overall partial.
real_issues: MAINNET confidential lane is unusable (HIGH, confirmed on-chain): Neo N3 mainnet kernel 0xf54d8584 oracleEncryptionPublicKey returns {"type":"Any"}/null. Clients cannot seal an X25519 envelope on mainnet, so confidential decrypt / private-transfer / neo-message reveal cannot be initiated on mainnet. Fix = publish the enclave's X25519 public key to the mainnet kernel (testnet already has kQvt…, version 7).

VRF randomness is NOT confirmed to be in-TEE (HIGH for a VRF/gaming user): fulfillment.js:917-918 uses relayer-local crypto.randomBytes(32) for random.generate; it only moves into the enclave when config.nitro.enclaveFulfill is true, whose DEFAULT is false and whose comment (config.js:431-437) says 'The live box runs flag-off'. If the prod relayer runs flag-off, VRF outcomes are generated by the relayer host process, not attested in the TEE — a trust downgrade vs the 'verifiable randomness in-TEE' claim. Verify MORPHEUS_RELAYER_ENCLAVE_FULFILL=true on the live box; if not set, VRF is host-tier.

Confidential decrypt cannot be exercised end-to-end right now (MEDIUM): public /oracle/decrypt = 503 runtime_route_unavailable, /oracle/query = 401, /prices = 503; runtime.meshmini.app 404s all unauth public paths except /health. The off-chain fetch/compute/decrypt API surface is not restored, so no live decrypt round-trip is provable. The validation graded confidential as 'works:yes' without a successful round-trip.

The on-chain published key (kQvt…) vs the enclave's actual KMS-materialized key are NOT proven equal (MEDIUM correctness risk): the public-key endpoint serves the value read from the on-chain contract (key_source neo_n3_contract), not the enclave's own key, so 'testnet decrypt coherent' is an inference, not a verified fact. The keystore holds yet a third value (PqIQ…). If the KMS key's public half ≠ kQvt, even testnet decrypt would silently fail. Needs a real sealed-envelope→decrypt round-trip once the route is restored.

Single-provider feeds have no cross-source sanity check on the anchored value (LOW, validation's own caveat is correct): most pairs default to twelvedata only, so aggregateQuotes median+25%-deviation rejection is informational; a bad single upstream quote would anchor on-chain. Add a 2nd provider per critical pair (NEO/GAS/BTC).

Low recent on-chain demand: newest fulfilled testnet kernel request (0x4b882e94) is ~15 days old (2026-05-31); mainnet kernel 0xf54d8584 has only 7 lifetime requests. Fulfillment works when requests arrive, but the 'live fulfillment' claim rests on relayer-internal counters not externally verifiable; on-chain evidence shows the request→fulfill path completing but with stale/low traffic.

Cross-repo citations: the validation's apps_using/correctness cite frontend files (useMorpheusDataFeed.ts, useSwapEngine.ts, neo-swap, neo-treasury) that live in neo-miniapps-platform, not this neo-morpheus-oracle repo (which only has apps/web). The behavior holds when verified in that repo, but the file:line citations are out-of-repo.

verdicts.reasoning: Adversarially re-verified the robustness lane and confirm works=yes. INDEPENDENTLY RE-RAN the 5 cited recovery-path test suites: 155/155 pass (fulfillment 79, request-processor 4, queue 39, state 10, neox 23) — the headline 155 is correct (the validation's per-suite attribution of 73 for fulfillment is a minor under-count; actual is 79; total unchanged). INDEPENDENTLY RE-RAN the on-chain reads myself: Neo N3 mainnet kernel 0xf54d8584 getTotalRequests=7 (HALT via mainnet1.neo.coz.io); Neo X mainnet kernel 0xeCFC1C65 totalRequests=0x1e=30 and oracleVerifier=0x622ae03bdb6d7e2a29be853c75d625bb25c0139c (computed correct selectors 0x8aea61dc/0x1ac0a23f via ethers; the validation's pre-derived values match exactly), chainId=0xba93=47763. oracle.meshmini.app/mainnet/v1/status returns operational topology; /v1/relayer returns runtime_route_unavailable (503) — confirming the validator's honest disclosure (issue #4) that the cited /metrics gauges (fulfill_success 29, dead_letter 0) are NOT publicly verifiable; only on-chain checkpoints + /v1/status are public. RE-READ every cited code path and all claims hold: error classification (fulfillment.js:142-171), full/equal jitter [0.5,1.0]*ceiling (state.js:420-428, fulfillment.js:173-184), atomic temp-file+rename state write (state.js:135-147), idempotency via confirmNeoN3FulfillExecution throw-on-timeout->re-broadcast (neo-n3.js:983-1018) and normalizeNeoXRevert RequestNotPending->already-fulfilled (neox.js:385-397), discovery/retry decoupling with cursor-not-advanced-on-discovery-failure (request-processor.js:159-206; last_block only set inside the success path at :188), per-signer runExclusive serialization + signer.reset nonce re-sync (neox.js:450-492), transport-only read failover that re-throws deterministic reverts (neox.js:139-155), Promise.race receipt deadline covering a never-settling wait (neox.js:414-431), dead-letter exhaustion recording (fulfillment.js:1074-1104), loop-level catch+failure-heartbeat+sleep (relayer.js:254-261), durable-claim cross-instance conflict handling (queue.js:313-409), and the pre-submit digest cross-check binding networkMagic/chainId for cross-deployment replay protection (fulfillment.js:509-520, 326/434/441). The B2 reconciliation test is genuinely meaningful, not a tautology (request-processor.test.mjs:114-176): it sets request 1 in retry under a DIFFERENT block-cursor key so key-dedup alone fails, request 2 already-processed, then the request-cursor scan returns 1,2,3 and asserts ONLY 3 is fulfilled — proving scan-by-request-id reconciliation picks up genuinely-missed requests while deduping tracked ones. All 4 low-severity issues are real and code-grounded.
real_issues: LOW (confirmed config.js:339-342): allowLocalClaimDuringBackoff defaults TRUE. During a Supabase outage the cross-instance idempotency claim goes offline and a SECOND relayer instance would be granted a local claim and could double-broadcast the same fulfillment. The code's own log at queue.js:339 literally states 'idempotency protection is OFF until Supabase recovers'. Mitigated by single-instance topology and the on-chain RequestNotPending/already-fulfilled revert backstop (a double-broadcast just FAULTs). Multi-instance deploys MUST set MORPHEUS_DURABLE_QUEUE_ALLOW_LOCAL_CLAIM_DURING_BACKOFF=false.

LOW (confirmed config.js:330): retryQueueLimit defaults to 0 = UNBOUNDED on the live box (a test is literally named 'enqueueRetryItem is unbounded (live-box default)'). A sustained ingestion burst + downstream outage grows the in-memory retry_queue with no ceiling, and the whole array re-serializes on every persist (memory + IO amplification on the single relayer host). Shedding-to-dead-letter only engages when an operator sets MORPHEUS_RELAYER_RETRY_QUEUE_LIMIT.

LOW (confirmed): no global circuit breaker / adaptive overload-shed. Recovery from a sustained dependency outage relies on per-tick caps (maxFreshEventsPerTick 32 / maxRetryEventsPerTick 16) + backoff + retry-queue limit, not a breaker that stops hammering a recovering RPC/Nitro. Acceptable for the cited ~30-request volume but would not scale-protect under a request storm coincident with an RPC/Nitro outage.

LOW (confirmed via HTTP probe): the relayer /metrics gauges that source the cited fulfill_success 29 / fulfill_failure 2 / dead_letter 0 / reconciliation_failures 0 numbers are NOT on the public trustless surface (oracle.meshmini.app/v1/relayer -> 503 runtime_route_unavailable; apps/web relayer-metrics is auth-gated). Only on-chain kernel request counts (re-verified advancing: N3=7, NeoX=30) and /v1/status (operational) are publicly confirmable. The validator correctly flagged this rather than over-claiming.

INFO (minor inaccuracy, not material): the validation attributes 73 tests to fulfillment.test.mjs; the actual count is 79. The cited TOTAL of 155 is correct (79+4+39+10+23) — the per-suite breakdown summed to 149, so the breakdown was internally inconsistent though the headline number checks out.
